content.text: Emergency Patch Procedures and Communication

An emergency patch is any security fix that must reach production faster than your normal release cycle. For Critical CVEs in GenAI dependencies, this means a 24-hour window from CVE publication to deployed fix. The pressure of that window creates the conditions where process shortcuts produce disasters — a rushed deploy that breaks model inference, a customer communication that reveals exploit details before the patch is live, or a patch that fixes the CVE but introduces a safety regression.
This sub-subtopic defines the step-by-step procedure, the communication templates, and the rollback contracts that allow emergency patches to be fast without being reckless.

attrs.alt: Emergency patch timeline across 24 hours with 6 phases and communication hierarchy by audience and time

content.text: When to Declare an Emergency Patch

Not every high-severity CVE requires an emergency patch procedure. The emergency declaration is triggered by specific criteria, and making the call too broadly burns out on-call engineers on false urgency.
Mandatory Emergency Patch Triggers
CVSS ≥ 9.0
in any dependency reachable from a production network boundary — no exceptions, regardless of exploitability assessment
Confirmed active exploitation in the wild
(CISA KEV listing, vendor advisory, credible threat intelligence) regardless of CVSS score
Deserialization vulnerability in an inference runtime
— ONNX Runtime, PyTorch, TorchServe, TensorRT — regardless of CVSS (these are RCE-class by category)
Safety guardrail bypass published
with a working proof-of-concept — a published jailbreak technique that defeats your production safety filters is a Critical event even with no CVE number
API key or model weight exposure vulnerability
in any SDK your production environment uses
Discretionary Emergency Patch (CVSS 7.0–8.9)
High severity vulnerabilities default to the 7-day window, but two conditions justify escalating them to emergency procedure:
The vulnerable code path is confirmed reachable
from untrusted user input in production — exploitability converts a theoretical high-severity into a practical one
A proof-of-concept exploit is publicly available
— PoC availability typically reduces time-to-exploitation from weeks to hours
The incident commander makes the escalation decision, documents the rationale, and notifies engineering leadership within 30 minutes of the decision.
The 24-Hour Emergency Patch Procedure
Phase 1: Detection and Triage (T+0 to T+1h)

The first hour sets the conditions for the rest of the response. Decisions made here — what the scope is, who is on point, whether a workaround exists — determine whether the next 23 hours are controlled or chaotic.
Automated CVE alert fires
— your CI/CD scanning tool detects the CVE in a production dependency and pages the on-call engineer. The clock starts here, not when a human reads the email.
Exploitability assessment (15 minutes)

— the on-call engineer answers three questions: (a) is the vulnerable package version in production? (b) is the vulnerable code path reachable from external input? (c) is there evidence of active exploitation? This assessment must be documented, not just mental.
P0 incident declared

— open the incident channel, page the incident commander and security engineer, update the incident tracker. Channel name convention:  {'type': 'text', 'text': '#p0-sec-YYYY-MM-DD', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}
Workaround assessment
— can the vulnerable component be disabled, rate-limited, or isolated while the patch is prepared? A workaround that reduces risk within 30 minutes buys time for a higher-quality patch
Notify CISO and engineering leadership
— do not wait until the patch is ready; leadership needs time to prepare for potential customer communication

# Incident channel bootstrap message (post immediately on P0 declaration)
# Template for #p0-sec-YYYY-MM-DD

## P0 Security Incident — [CVE-YYYY-XXXXX]

**Status:** ACTIVE
**Declared:** [timestamp UTC]
**Incident Commander:** @[name]
**Security Lead:** @[name]
**On-Call Engineer:** @[name]

**Summary:** [CVE-YYYY-XXXXX] — [one-line description of vulnerability]
**Affected package:** [package-name]==[version]
**CVSS:** [score] ([severity])
**Exploitability:** [Confirmed reachable / Assessed not reachable / Unknown]
**Active exploitation:** [Yes / No / Unknown]
**Workaround available:** [Yes: [description] / No]

**Timeline:**
- [T+0] CVE alert received, on-call paged
- [T+15] Exploitability assessment complete
- [T+30] P0 declared, IC assigned

**Next update:** [T+60]
Phase 2: Patch Development (T+1h to T+4h)
The hotfix branch is isolated from the main development branch to prevent the emergency patch from picking up unreviewed feature changes that could introduce new issues.

# Cut the hotfix branch from the current production tag
git fetch origin
git checkout -b hotfix/CVE-YYYY-XXXXX origin/main

# Apply the dependency update
pip install --upgrade vulnerable-package==FIXED_VERSION
pip freeze | grep vulnerable-package >> requirements.txt

# Or for a code-level fix:
# Apply the upstream patch directly if a package release is not yet available
git cherry-pick <upstream-fix-commit>

# Document what was changed in the branch
git commit -m "security: patch CVE-YYYY-XXXXX in [package-name]

Upgrades [package-name] from [old-version] to [fixed-version].
CVE-YYYY-XXXXX: [one-line description]
Exploitability in this codebase: [assessed reachable / not reachable]
Patch window: Critical (24h)
"

Two-engineer review is required for emergency patches — not optional. The second engineer's job is specifically to check that the patch does not introduce new problems, not to assess whether the vulnerability fix is correct (that is the first engineer's responsibility).
Phase 3: Testing (T+4h to T+8h)
Full regression testing cannot always complete in the time available. The emergency test protocol runs a targeted subset that covers the patched component plus critical path smoke tests.
Test Category
What It Covers
Fail Action
Targeted unit tests
Tests for the patched component's public API surface
Block deploy, escalate to IC
Integration smoke test
End-to-end happy path: user request → LLM response → return
Block deploy
Golden prompt comparison
20 representative prompts: output must not change materially
Human review before deploy
Safety regression
Red-team prompt set must still be blocked by guardrails
Block deploy, treat as safety incident
Performance baseline
P99 latency must be within 10% of pre-patch baseline
Deploy with monitoring, alert if breached

The golden prompt comparison may show changed outputs after an LLM SDK update. Changed outputs are not automatically a block — they require human judgment about whether the change is a regression or neutral. Assign the ML engineer to review and make a documented call within 30 minutes.
Phase 4: Deployment (T+8h to T+16h)

Emergency patches use a canary deployment pattern even under time pressure. A canary deploy that reveals a critical regression costs 30 minutes. A full rollout that reveals the same regression triggers a rollback that costs hours and a second customer communication explaining why the patch broke things.

# Emergency canary deployment sequence
# Adjust percentages and wait times to your infrastructure

deploy_sequence:
  - environment: staging
    traffic_pct: 100
    wait_minutes: 15
    monitor:
      - error_rate < 0.1%
      - p99_latency < baseline_p99 * 1.1
      - safety_block_rate within 5% of baseline

  - environment: production
    traffic_pct: 10
    wait_minutes: 10
    monitor:
      - error_rate < 0.1%
      - no new safety bypasses

  - environment: production
    traffic_pct: 50
    wait_minutes: 10
    monitor:
      - same as above

  - environment: production
    traffic_pct: 100
    wait_minutes: 30   # final observation window before closing P0
    monitor:
      - same as above
Rollback Contract

Before deploying, the incident commander must define the rollback trigger: specific, observable conditions that automatically initiate rollback without a committee decision. Ambiguous rollback criteria cause hesitation when a rollback is needed quickly.
Rollback triggers (any one is sufficient):
(1) Error rate increases > 1% above pre-patch baseline within 5 minutes of canary deploy
(2) Any safety regression: a prompt that was previously blocked is now passing through
(3) Inference latency P99 increases > 25% above baseline
(4) Any model output that was stable in golden prompt comparison is now meaningfully different in production traffic sampling

# Rollback command — must be executable without additional approval
# Pre-test this command before the emergency deploy begins

# For dependency version rollback:
git checkout production-pre-patch-tag -- requirements.txt
pip install -r requirements.txt
# Rebuild and deploy the previous image

# For container-based deploys:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/genai-api -n production

# Confirm rollback completed:
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-api -n production
# Verify traffic is back on previous version:
curl -s https://api.example.com/version | jq .version
Communication Templates and Timing

Communication during an emergency patch has two failure modes: too early (customers panic before you have anything to tell them) and too late (customers discover the vulnerability from external sources and lose trust). The timing structure below threads this needle.
T+2h: Initial Customer Advisory

The initial advisory is sent to all affected customers within 2 hours of the P0 declaration. At this point the patch is not yet deployed — the purpose is to notify customers that you are aware and responding, not to disclose technical details that could help an attacker.

Subject: Security Advisory — [Your Company Name] Action In Progress

Dear [Customer Name],

We are writing to inform you that we have identified a security vulnerability
in a third-party component used in [Product Name]. We are actively working to
deploy a fix and expect to complete patching within 24 hours.

At this time, we have no evidence that this vulnerability has been exploited
or that any customer data has been affected. We are monitoring continuously.

What you need to do: Nothing at this time. This is an infrastructure-level
patch that does not require any action on your part.

We will send a follow-up advisory within 24 hours confirming the patch has
been deployed and providing additional technical detail.

If you have questions, contact your Customer Success Manager or reach us
at security@[yourdomain].com.

[Company Name] Security Team
T+20-24h: Patch Deployed Advisory
Once production is fully patched and the observation window has cleared, send the post-deploy advisory. This is the advisory where you provide technical detail.

Subject: Security Advisory Update — Patch Deployed: [CVE-YYYY-XXXXX]

Dear [Customer Name],

We have successfully deployed a security patch for [CVE-YYYY-XXXXX] across
all [Product Name] infrastructure. Patching completed at [timestamp UTC].

Vulnerability summary:
  CVE ID: CVE-YYYY-XXXXX
  Affected component: [package-name] versions [X.Y.Z] through [A.B.C]
  Description: [One-paragraph non-technical description of what the
    vulnerability is and what an attacker could theoretically do]
  Fixed in: [package-name] version [FIXED_VERSION]

Impact to your environment:
  [Choose the appropriate statement:]
  - We have found no evidence that this vulnerability was exploited in any
    customer environment, including yours.
  - OR: During our investigation, we found evidence of [describe impact]. We
    will contact you separately with details specific to your account.

Action required:
  No action is required on your part. The patch has been applied to all
  infrastructure automatically.

We have scheduled a post-mortem to assess our detection and response
processes. If you would like to discuss this incident, please contact
your CSM or reply to this email.

[Company Name] Security Team
Communication for Safety-Related Emergency Patches

If the emergency patch addresses a safety guardrail bypass — a published jailbreak technique that circumvents content filtering — the customer communication requires additional care. Customers deploying GenAI in regulated or sensitive contexts need to know whether the bypass was exploited before the patch was applied.

Subject: Safety Advisory — Guardrail Update Deployed

Dear [Customer Name],

We have deployed an update to the safety filtering layer in [Product Name]
in response to a newly published prompt injection technique.

What changed: A research paper published on [date] described a technique
that could cause our safety filters to misclassify certain inputs. We have
updated our guardrail models and rule patterns to address this technique.
This update does not involve a software vulnerability (no CVE) — it is a
content-level response to evolving adversarial techniques.

Did this affect your environment:
  We reviewed [N] days of traffic in your environment (the period during
  which this technique was publicly known) and found:
  - [0 / N] instances of the described pattern in your input traffic.
  - [0 / N] cases where safety filters produced an unexpected result.

What we are doing:
  - Guardrail models updated and deployed as of [timestamp].
  - Weekly monitoring for new bypass variants is now in place.

[Company Name] Safety Team
Post-Mortem at T+72h

Every emergency patch requires a post-mortem, regardless of outcome. A clean patch deployed on time is still a learning opportunity — what would have made it faster? What gaps in monitoring delayed detection?
Post-Mortem Template

# Post-Mortem: CVE-YYYY-XXXXX — [Package Name]
Date: [date]
Incident Commander: [name]
Duration: T+0 to T+[N]h (patch deployed to all production)

## Summary
[2–3 sentence description: what the vulnerability was, what we patched, outcome]

## Timeline
| Time (UTC) | Event |
|-----------|-------|
| HH:MM | CVE published |
| HH:MM | Automated alert fired |
| HH:MM | On-call paged |
| HH:MM | P0 declared |
| HH:MM | Patch authored |
| HH:MM | Testing complete |
| HH:MM | Production fully patched |
| HH:MM | Customer advisory sent |

## Detection Gap
Time from CVE publication to first internal alert: [N] minutes/hours.
Was this within our target (<60 minutes for Critical)? [Yes/No]
Root cause of gap (if any): [e.g., CVE was published on weekend, scanning ran on 6-hour cadence]

## What Went Well
- [list]

## What Went Poorly
- [list]

## Action Items
| Action | Owner | Due |
|--------|-------|-----|
| [e.g., reduce scan cadence from 6h to 1h for Critical deps] | @engineer | YYYY-MM-DD |
Metrics Reviewed in Every Patch Post-Mortem
Time from CVE publication to alert
— target < 60 minutes for Critical CVEs. Gaps here indicate monitoring cadence or coverage problems
Time from alert to P0 declaration
— target < 30 minutes. Long gaps indicate paging or triage process friction
Time from P0 to patch deployed
— target < 20 hours (leaves buffer inside 24-hour window). Gaps here indicate testing, review, or deployment pipeline bottlenecks
Number of customers who contacted us before receiving the advisory
— any inbound contact from customers about a vulnerability before your advisory went out means your communication was too slow
Rollback events
— any rollback is a process failure in testing, and the root cause should be identified specifically
Break-Glass Access Controls

Emergency patches often require deploying to production without the normal approval gates — a two-person production deploy policy, a mandatory 48-hour bake time in staging, or a change advisory board approval. Break-glass procedures allow bypassing these controls with documented justification.
The break-glass policy must define:
Who can authorize break-glass
— typically the incident commander at VP level or above, or a designated security officer. Not the on-call engineer alone.
What must be documented before bypassing
— the specific control being bypassed, the specific reason, and the compensating action (e.g., 'bypassing 48h staging bake; compensating with 30-minute canary in production')
Automatic post-incident review
— every break-glass use triggers a mandatory line item in the post-mortem asking whether the bypass was necessary and whether the compensating action was adequate
Audit log
— all break-glass authorizations and the actions taken under them must be logged immutably for compliance and later review
Break-glass is a pressure relief valve, not a shortcut culture.

Teams that normalize break-glass lose the protection it is designed to provide. If break-glass is used more than once per quarter, the normal patch pipeline is too slow and should be optimized instead of bypassed.
Summary

Emergency patch procedures exist to compress the normal patch cycle from weeks to 24 hours without introducing the regressions that haste causes. The structure — a declared incident, phased canary deploy, pre-defined rollback triggers, and tiered customer communication — is what allows that compression to be safe.

For GenAI applications, two elements distinguish emergency patching from traditional software: the golden prompt comparison test gate (output stability, not just functional correctness) and the safety regression suite (guardrail bypasses are a distinct failure class that standard tests do not cover). Both gates must pass before a GenAI patch reaches production, even under a 24-hour deadline.

The post-mortem at T+72h closes the loop. Without it, emergency patches are isolated events. With it, each one makes detection faster, patching smoother, and communication more precise — until the 24-hour window becomes routine rather than harrowing.
